turns.name: HIRSCHHORN
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: raised before this Court is whether the defendants were denied their rights to a fair trial and due process of law by virtue of the mere presence of cameras in the courtroom over their obj ection. The appellants contend that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: This is any camera? televised on the evening news of selected portions of the trial.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How about still photographs?

turns.name: HIRSCHHORN
turns.text: The record will not reflect the presence of any still photographic camera present during the proceeding.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But this was both audio and visual?

turns.name: HIRSCHHORN
turns.text: Yes, Mr. Chief Justice.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And the record reflects the location of the camera?

turns.name: HIRSCHHORN
turns.text: Yes, as a matter of fact, In the Appendix to the appellees' reply brief at page A36, you have a picture of the courtroom in which the circle with the "x" in it reflects the presence of the television camera.

turns.name: HIRSCHHORN
turns.text: No, it was not, Mr. Justice Rehnquist. Motions were repeatedly made to sequester the jury and denied. The record will reflect that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But the District Court of Appeal in Florida found against you on your claim of prejudice from the pretrial publicity?

turns.name: HIRSCHHORN
turns.text: That is correct, and that is perhaps because I misperceived the issue in the District Court of Appeal when I argued it. This is not a pretrial publicity case like Gannett. This is not an effort to close the court­ as in Richmond Newspapers, to close the trial. This is not a closure case. The defendants here do not seek whatsoever to Court of Appeal was correct in finding on the record there was no measurable prejudice. The issue we raised there and the issue we raise here is that human nature and our under­ standing of human nature and common sense tells us that being on television is different than any other type of public .trial and that although we may not be able to measure with specific­ ity the prejudice, behavioral scientists tell us that people act differently, posture differently, pose differently when they know they're on TV.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Was this kind of evidence introduced in

turns.name: HIRSCHHORN
turns.text: No, we were not permitted to intro­ duce any evidence. We were never granted a de novo hearing on this.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Did you tender such evidence? occurred was, we filed a motion to exclude cameras in the When we went back to trial we were then placed in a posture where the judge said he had a mandate, he could not even ask whether it was mandatory or discretionary. There is no evidence that there was in fact prejudice per se because we were not afforded an evidentiary hearing, nor do I believe an evidentiary hearing would have been necessary. An evidentiary hearing was not necessary in Estelle v. Williams, an evidentiary hearing was not necessary in Jack- son v. Denno when this Court determined that it is inherently prejudicial to a defendant for a jury to determine the volun­

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You're saying in effect that we as pre­ sumably not behavioral psychologists but as lay people in that field can determine on our own whether it's inherently preju­ dicial that something be done?

turns.name: HIRSCHHORN
turns.text: I am saying that, Mr. Justice Rehnquist, that your understanding of human nature, as you did in Turney, Turner, Estes, it is not necessary to have an that is accurate, but I'm not sure that ham is the issue. I know television will make a cocky witness more cocky. I cer­ tainly know it will make a timid witness more timid.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Now, when you say you know these things, do you get them by osmosis or how do you -- I realize we all "know" certain things that we can't demonstrate, but when you say you know, this is from your own experience as a litigation lawyer?

turns.name: HIRSCHHORN
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, I think we know that no judge would not be fair and impartial where te imposed $12. But this Court in Turney said, that procedure is inher­ ently prejudicial because it creates the possibility, the probability of a denial of a fair and an impartial trial.

turns.name: HIRSCHHORN
turns.text: Oh, yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And so we have a set of state courts say­ ing that your submission is erroneous?

turns.name: HIRSCHHORN
turns.text: We have a set of state courts say­ ing that the public's -- the method of access is more important in the context of sunshine in the government than individuals' rights to a fair and impartial trial.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You mean,..did the Florida courts say that, yes, there is some inherent prejudice to the defendant? They said there was none, didn't they?

turns.name: HIRSCHHORN
turns.text: They said there was nothing demon­ strated as applied.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So we'd have a set of findings against you?

turns.name: HIRSCHHORN
turns.text: No, and yes. I did not make an offer of proof, and the trial judge said, it won't make any difference if you do. The rule is mandatory, but I'll certify the question as one of great public import. Let the Florida Supreme Court hear it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, then, to pursue Mr. Justice Rehnquiit's question, and then several others, are we to rely, for example, on -- to take some current things -- a question and answer interview by George Ball in the current issue of Newsweek, something like that, and a column by a columnist in the Washington Post, Ellen Goodman, who said that television tends to distort the processes that it depicts? Is that the kind of thing we as a reviewing court can rely on?

turns.name: HIRSCHHORN
turns.text: .. Well, when you review Jackson v. Denno and Estelle v. Williams, and Rideau and Turner, there

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Hirschhorn, do you rely on the Estes case at all?

turns.name: HIRSCHHORN
turns.text: Yes, I do.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What was the evidence of actual prejudice in that case?

turns.name: HIRSCHHORN
turns.text: None. No evidence whatsoever.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Didn't the Supreme Court just take judi­ cial notice of everything, at least in Chief Justice Warren's opinion and in the majority opinion? They had no hearing on prejudice, did they? the Estes case the only thing that was televised was the

turns.name: HIRSCHHORN
turns.text: No, no.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Where is the camera?

turns.name: HIRSCHHORN
turns.text: The camera is that circle with the "X" in it. "Electric" is so that the cameraman would know where to plug his cord in.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I see. But the camera is over in the pub­ lic seats section, is that it?

turns.name: HIRSCHHORN
turns.text: Yes. Right behind the bar.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What's the evidence that the participants in the trial and the jury knew that they were being televised? it, and --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, no. What is the evidence in the record that they knew the proceedings were being televised?

turns.name: HIRSCHHORN
turns.text: The record will reflect that during judge quickly corrected me that it was "a camera," the same camera.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Hirschhorn, I would almost conclude from your submission that you would urge that neither the defendant nor the prosecution, together, could stipulate to have cameras, .in ' the room?

turns.name: HIRSCHHORN
turns.text: That is correct. And unlike Richmond Newspapers, where the prosecution and defense stipu­ lated and the judge granted the stipulation, there was no discretion whatsoever. The judge had to do it -- rule excluding them, no matter whether there's any objection by anybody or not?

turns.name: HIRSCHHORN
turns.text: No. That would be asking for too much. I think that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, who could -- whose objection must We say that where a defendant objects to the presence of cameras: in the courtroom, his objection is sufficient -- if a witness: -- because the method of gathering news is in­ herently prejudicial -- if a witness' or a juror objects, then the judge -- the per se rule is where the defendant objects. If a witness or a juror objects, or a lawyer objects, then the judge has to do the balancing test. Which is more important at this point, the witness or juror's right of privacy, if there is one in a courtroom, and I don't think that there is, against the media'a method of gathering news in this case. kind of a situation you were just describing would have any special right to demand that television be admitted?

turns.name: HIRSCHHORN
turns.text: No, and in fact, in fact there are

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, but this case is in quite a different posture from either De Pasquale or Richmond News­ papers, in that the state has opted for television coverage. In those two cases the state opted against coverage. You make it sound as if the Constitution covers the entire 100 percent of the spectrum, without any state latitude.

turns.name: HIRSCHHORN
turns.text: Mr. Justice Rehnquist, the state did not opt. The Florida Supreme Court enacted a rule without the consent of the governed.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, that's the state, for our purposes, when the Florida Supreme Court opts it for a rule.

turns.name: HIRSCHHORN
turns.text: Well, Mr. Rehnquist, your views on state's rights are well known to me. I have read and reread your dissent, and I suggest to you that the state under state's instructions, they -- every trial, federal and state, at the conclusion of a case the jury is instructed, you, the jury, are: the sole judges of the credibility and believability of the witnesses. You, the jury, shall determine the deameanor of the witness and the witness's ability to know and remember the facts about which he testifies. How do we know that the witness is sweating because on cross-examination the defense attorney finally hit a nerve center that causes the witness to wonder whether he's going to recant, or is he sweating because of the presence of cameras ir. the courtroom? We don't know that. But we certainly know, we certainly know that the greater the exposure -- and behavioral scientists have told us, and we know -- that as in Brown v. Board of Education and the Brandeis brief that was filed, that behavioral scientists tell us what human nature and common sense tells us. Mr. Justice Stewart and Mr. Justice Harlan and those who joined were, reluctant to close the doors on the grounds that we need this novel form of experimentation to see. Well, we've had this novel form of experimentation, an experiment without direction, an experiment without guidance, an experiment with­ out controls. And I don't think anybody on this bench can stand here and say, when 23 percent of the jurors or 30 percent of the witnesses who finally responded to questionnaires which were subjective in and of themselves, admitted an awareness of cameras in the courtroom. That gets right down to the whole purpose behind due process of law. That gets us back to In re: Murchison. That gets us back to why having a bailiff who is also a deputy sheriff and a witness, but who swears he didn't say anything to the jurors, consent of the defendant. There are seventeen in addition who do require the consent of the defendant.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So, if you were in one of those 17 states you wouldn't be here?

turns.name: HIRSCHHORN
turns.text: That's correct. I absolutely would not be here because of the waiver principles.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Under the Florida procedure can the com­ plaining witness -- does the trial judge have discretion to exclude any pictures if the complaining witness is testifying?

turns.name: HIRSCHHORN
turns.text: Yes, Mr. Chief Justice, at page 779 that says, 'The presiding judge may exclude the electronic media qualitative difference. Do we really have to have a behavior­ al scientist tell us that when a juvenile or a woman is vic­ timized by a sexual assault, she doesn't need to testify in front of a television camera to further and deepen her humil­ ity? Do we need evidence in the record of that? I suggest not.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Hirschhorn, does the record tell us why the portions of the trial that were televised and no others? In other words, why the defense case was not tele­ vised? understand, there's one minicamera in the courtroom. Does it tell us what it picked up in the camera? Is it just focused on the witness, or is. it on the whole courtroom, or how does it, what is the picture that is televised, do you know?

turns.name: HIRSCHHORN
turns.text: Well, this record won't tell, be­ cause I was busy trying the case. And I have to presume from the clips I saw on the evening news that :they focused on Mr. Sion, the State's main witness, giving his key testimony. And they focused on jurors giving responses and they focused

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is the cameraman in the courtroom who fixed camera is what I'm trying to say.

turns.name: HIRSCHHORN
turns.text: It's a fixed camera on a tripod swi­ vel, but I think they use a zoom lens so they don't move the the public sees but the fact that the jury knows that the camera's present just for that portion of the. State's direct examination, but is not present for any portion of cross or the defendant's case. And I suggest that behavioral scientists common sense, and human nature tell us that if a television camera picks up one part of a trial but not another, it's be­ cause someone has made a judgment that that part is more important than the other.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That has nothing to do with the defen­ dant's rights, though, does it? respect.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What the public sees is no part of this case .

turns.name: HIRSCHHORN
turns.text: I'm sorry, I misunderstood your question. Of course. I was trying to draw that distinction.

turns.name: HIRSCHHORN
turns.text: That is the only aspect. The mar­ shal has advised me I have about six minutes. I'd like to re­ serve that for rebuttal, if I might.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Mr. Fox.

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: I will be arguing the case in chief here this morn­ ing. The Attorney General of Florida will address broader policy considerations with respect to the 17 states and the conference of chief justices who have submitted amicus briefs in support of the State's position. The State's position in this case is quite clear. The defendants received a fair trial. The defendants, on the based specifically on the one question the Court granted cer­ tiorari to consider.

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: It is a plurality opinion in which only four, members of the Court joined in a rule, per se rule against judgment of the Court, however, reversing --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I'm talking about the judgment of the Court on the facts of the case. Why isn't that directly in point? In fact, why isn't that a stronger case because there the jury was sequestered?

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: It is a stronger case, Your Honor. And

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: A stronger case for allowing the televi­ sion .

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: Pardon me, Your Honor? Mr. Justice Stevens, the court focused upon pre-trial hearings, The cables snaking all over the floor --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I asked you, why wasn't the holding spe­ cifically in point here?

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: It is not specifically on point, Your Honor, because of the fact circumstance there. The facts there do not remotely relate to --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In what respect? How are they different, other than the fact that the jury was sequestered there?

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: The facts in that case, the --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I'm talking about the trial, too; not the pre-trial hearing.

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: Your Honor, I would submit to you that the trial was a separate and distinctly serene proceeding. In fact, we would submit that the only televised portion of that

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: But, Your Honor, a reading of the Clark opinion, a reading of the Warren, Douglas, and Goldberg opin­ ions , they concentrate entirely upon the pre-trial hearings, and the infection which preceded the trial. They do not dis­ cuss the serenity which prevailed at the trial. They do not separate --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That.'s all that was actually an issue in the Court's judgment, and the Court, as a Court, not a plurality but a Court, five people reversed the conviction in the case because of the presence of television at the trial,

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: Your Honor, the --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So why isn't my brother Stevens exactly correct in his question?

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, that becomes a question, then. What's a notorious case? I would submit to you the Estes case was a notorious case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, do the Florida rules apply to all trials ?

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, it certainly does. I think if the Court wants --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Would it apply to a drunken driving case?

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: Pardon me?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Would it apply to a drunken driving case?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And would it apply to a misdemeanor case?

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, any trial. Or --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It would apply to a spitting-on-the-

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: On the pre-trial publicity point that's certainly true, but that was not what was decided.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you think there'd be any tendency on the part of the local television station to televise a case involving an action to quiet title to real estate?

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, as the Wisconsin brief notes, television cameras have been at zoning appeals, at traffic appeals, at any sort of civil proceedings --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The action to quiet title that I hypothe­ sized. Do you think there'd be any interest in a television station or in any of the people who watch television to see that kind of a trial? it is possible that a circumstance could arise where a quiet policy of the stations cannot be governed by this court or any court. It may be they don't have the time or the expense to put a camera in every courtroom and that type of case would probably certainly have a very low priority.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Let me ask you this hypothetical question since we have no empirical evidence in the record. Suppose in this particular case the television after showing whatever it was they showed, the opening statement and the closing argu­ media would result in a rescission of the rule. Now, this is a rule --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If they abuse the First Amendment, the Supreme Court's going to go after them?

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: No, Your Honor --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is that the way you read that rule?

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: Pardon me?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is that the way you read that rule?

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: Your Honor, in the final paragraph of the opinion, the Court says that the past abuses of the press are states in its final paragraph of the Post-Newsweek opinion. And I think, quite clearly, the Florida Supreme Court has left it open for abuses by the press. And I think in the event that contempt for doing that?

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: I think the judicial control which has beer left in the Florida trial courts by the rule. It's quite clear1 in the rule that the judicial control and discretion, to con­ trol the camera and the media and disputes among the media, is strictly restricted into the trial court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Could they? Could the Court properly fine them in contempt'for having shown only the prosecution's case and then taken an instant poll on guilty or innocent?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Have they done, so since the rule was adopted':

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, they certainly have.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What considerations impel a court to make that ruling?

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: Well, the rule itself states quite clearly the grounds relating to the difference in the effect upon the witness as opposed to the general public, and the difference relating to the type of media coverage. As, for example, the trial court may exclude the electronic media but not other types of media.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I would distinguish, of course, the printed press media. You have different authorities that con­ trol that. But I understand your answer to be that the Court does have authority to exclude all cameras from the entire hearing on the issue, and that the motion showed a prima facie case but then there should be an evidentiary hearing. That case is currently on appeal to the Florida Supreme Court. We're in a frontier area here, Your Honor. There have been very, very few cases with respect to closure or the media appealing a closure or the defendant appealing from an issue where closure was denied.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Have you had a case yet where a witness after being sworn but before taking the stand simply said to the judge, I've been camera-shy all my life, I just don't think I can possibly testify fairly and honestly with cameras on me ?

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: We have a case involving a defendant, Green it was in the best interests of justice? HR. FOX: Yes, Your Honor. I think a full discretion should rest in the trial court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Suppose you had a camera-shy lawyer?

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, that relates to another issue, I think, an issue which this Court has occasionally had a chance to address, Mr. Chief Justice, involving the quality of representation, the quality of trial court judges, and so forth. We submit that the exposure of the trial courts and the attorneys to public scrutiny I think will have an educa­ will clearly be demonstrated in front of the cameras. I think a trial judge who is not up on the law will clearly be demon­ strated by the actual and real picture of what's going on. incompetent, is going to be incompetent, a showoff, or a strutter, whether the cameras are there or not.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But that doesn't answer the question I put and that I thought Mr. Justice Powell was probing at. What if the very presence of the camera as with the camera-shy witness, impairs the functioning of a highly self-conscious lawyer, if you can hypothesize that?

turns.name: FOX
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, I'd submit to you that that is precisely what we've argued in our brief. We should proceed on a case by case basis. In the event that is shown, in the event that a defendant files a 3.850 proceeding in Florida collaterally attacking the competency of his counsel, then we should proceed on a case-by-case basis. We should not predicate our decision in this case upon conjecture, but upon the underlying philosophy and restricted scope of Justice Clark's opinion when he said, and I quote from that opinion briefly, "Finally, we should not be deterred from making the constitutional judgment which this case demands by the prospect that the day may come when television will have become so commonplace an affair in the daily life of the average person as to dissipate all reasonable likelihood that its use in courtrooms may disparage the judicial process. If and when that day arrives, the constitutional judgment called for now would of course be subject to reexamination." would give very serious consideration to the claim of a lawyer who said he was used to practising in a courtroom that only first .in Yankee Stadium?

turns.name: SMITH
turns.text: No, sir. This Court, obviously, wouldn't accommodate --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's about the only way you can get a public trial in New York, isn't it?

turns.name: SMITH
turns.text: I'm sorry, Your Honor?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's about the only way you could get a "public" trial in New York, would be a building twice the size of Yankee Stadium.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How do you differentiate this from Yankee

turns.name: SMITH
turns.text: The courtroom size will accommodate the number of people that the particular courtroom designated can take . There are a lot more people watching. submission improve the quality of justice that's administered in a courtroom?

turns.name: SMITH
turns.text: The experience that we have had to date in Florida, and we have certainly had our share of highly sen­ sational publicized trials, has been that the presence of cameras in the courtroom has indeed improved that process . The fact is, in the Zamora trial, which was a very sensational, highly publicized capital case, was televised gavel-to-gavel in Florida, and portions of that trial were shown worldwide. The experience in that proceeding was that for the most part the proceedings on the TV monitor, rather than going back and forth in the courtroom carrying messages to each other. The Bundy trial is overturned, wouldn't your rule be invalid in a rather large constitutional rights.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I know, but I just asked you about the category of trial such as Justice Harlan described in his opinion in that case. He thought there was a type of trial in which television would be per se prejudicial.

turns.name: SMITH
turns.text: I would not --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Now, in that category of trial, wouldn't your rule be invalid in at least some cases under Estes?

turns.name: SMITH
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. The case could cer­ tainly come back before this Court, where a trial judge did not exercise the proper control of the proceedings and let it --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That isn't Justice Harlan's holding, is

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, isn't that almost self-defining? Wouldn't any trial which television was interested in covering be by definition a notorious or sensational trial?

turns.name: SMITH
turns.text: No, Your Honor, the experience in Florida is that every news station in our state has a courthouse beat and it is common in the TV news programs and in the newspapers in Florida that what is going on at the courthouse is reported.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What about the Zamora case?

turns.name: SMITH
turns.text: Excuse me, Your Honor?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you think Justice Harlan would have thought that the Zamora case fell within his definition of a notorious trial?

turns.name: SMITH
turns.text: I do not, Your Honor, and I would like Judge Baker, who was the trial judge in that case, at the request of the Florida Supreme Court made a very detailed media covers every trial. I only -- the major newspapers in our state and TV stations have reporters on the courthouse beat, and that normally in the news segment there will be a minute or so every night for what is happening at the court­ house. It will not be every case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, it obviously can't be.

turns.name: SMITH
turns.text: Absolutely. It would not be every case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Are you telling us, Mr. Attorney General, that broadcasting two minutes and 52 seconds, was it, or 55 seconds of the prosecution'scase only, just one side of the of the administration of justice in Florida?

turns.name: SMITH
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, in particularly a sensational type trial, the media will cover that event, it later the jury finds the defendants in a particular case not guilty, that the jurors will be subject to criticism of their neighbors and their friends and their associates for a verdict of not guilty, when they have seen evidence only of guilt? Is that a possibility that the courts should take into account?

turns.name: SMITH
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, that certainly is a possibility, but that possibility exists and does happen whe­ ther cameras are present in the courtroom or not.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In any event, Mr. Attorney General, we don't need to, and we mustn't, decide 'Whether or not this is a is constitutionally permissible.

turns.name: SMITH
turns.text: That's correct. Whether or not there is inherent prejudice would rather put in their presence -- in what may be a disagreement between the Honorable Chief Justice and Justice Rehnquist -- the Constitution in the sense of the administration of justice, you know, within a state and what kind of rules a supreme court may adopt for that state, a state supreme court so long as they don't rise to a federal constitutional violation would have discretion to adopt what­ ever rules they might deem proper. The Florida rule authorizing the presence of the electronic media in the courtroom does that well within consti­ has given states to experiment with novel ideas. The experi­ The most recent Roper report tells us there are more than 90 million TV sets in this country, that that television in the average household is on some 6-1/2 hours a day. If I could comment briefly on the experience in the Zamora trial which was a highly publicized trial, as I say, in this country and worldwide. Judge Baker took note of Justice Clark's concern about the red light on the camera. Obviously the state of art in cameras has changed. We don't have lights on the cameras in our courtrooms that indicate when they're on or off, and there is only one camera in the courtroom. Another concern in the Estes case was the fact that the trial judge had to hold no less than five hearings about the pre-trial coverage to referee disputes between the media. Under the Florida rule the pooling arrangement as to who will operate the one camera that is present in the courtroom, those decisions are worked out among the media themselves. The court is not called upon to referee any disputes. If there are dis­ putes in that regard, then cameras are not present in the courtroom. present in their courtrooms. In eight states those proceed­ ings may be televised without the consent of the defendant. In three states appellate proceedings are televised without the consent of the defendant's lawyer in that case. In the remaining states, some 16, television coverage is allowed with the defendant's consent. States have experimented in this area successfully. We urge this Court to uphold the Florida Supreme Court rule in the conviction below. Thank you. General. Mr. Hirschhorn, do you have anything further?

turns.name: HIRSCHHORN
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please; the Court: Being around television and watching television is But Zamora's co-defendant is here, Your Honors. Agrella is here on cert, which this Court has been holding, unless it did something with it this past week. Agrella was a separate co-defendant who said, because his co-defendant didn't object, he couldn't get a fair trial. That's a collat­ eral matter that has to be addressed. Mr. Justice Harlan said, if every person in Yankee Stadium sat stonily silent, it's not the same as the decorum and the atmosphere of a courtroom. Showing two minutes and 55 seconds, showing filmed highlights, is what precipitated, I suggest, and it has been argued in the brief, the disaster in Miami in the wake of what we call the McDuffie verdict. In haec verba, the due process clause, Mr. Justice Rehnquist, I suggest to you prohibits the State of Florida from doing this, and I suggest that the Declaration of Independence, July 4, 1776, requires the consent of the governed.

turns.name: HIRSCHHORN
turns.text: Just its history. I agree.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I don't imagine the ratings would have been too good on that. show. Putting that to one side for a moment, one thing troubles me about your argument, Mr. Hirschhorn, on witnesses. If the television camera does adversely affect the witness's ability to testify in a per­ suasive way, effective way, here they only showed prosecution withesses, so whatever harm was done to this trial process presumably would have been prejudicial to the prosecution.

turns.name: HIRSCHHORN
turns.text: Not necessarily. Because of the jury's impact. Perhaps this witness was not affected, some­ how. But to the jury sitting back there in their jury room, they can be thinking and we can assume that they are correctly thinking about which witness's testimony was more important or more relevant. And I think they can say --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But does the jury know which part is being televised?

turns.name: HIRSCHHORN
turns.text: Oh, yes. They know which --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Because, as I understand it, there's no red light anymore. present during jury selection, and the camera was only pre­ sent during Mr. Sion's testimony. The camera was not present during other witness, during defense testimony, during closing argument. Palm Beach Newspapers -- I urge the Court to read Palm Beach Newspapers; that was cited by my opponent. Because Judge Scholts said that .the state attorneys who labor every day with witnesses, that state attorney filed two affida­ vits saying he got two witnesses who don't want to testify. They're in fear, they're in concern of their life. Judge Scholts excluded the cameras, not from the trial, but from the rule does not permit blanket non-televising of the trial, just the successfully objecting participant. And on appeal, the 4th District Court of Appeal said. Mr. trial judge, an affidavit's not enough because the media has to be given a chance to confront, cross-examine the affi­ davit. That's an insufficient showing, just because two peo­ the jury has this sole and exclusive province to evaluate the witness's testimony. The First Amendment has yielded in Zurcher to the search warrant. The First Amendment has yieldec in Branzburg to the grand jury. There is no battle here. It is just an effort to preserve the very nerve center of the factfinding process, the life-blood of the system, the defendants' right to a fair and impartial trial. Both the First and the Sixth Amendment rights can be observed without violence to either. There is no harm what­ soever if other traditional methods of gathering news, which were in effect when the Constitution was written, are used.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is that the way you distinguish the writ­ ten word from the television picture?
